quick_facts.label: What this title means
quick_facts.value: This is a state-specific, combined entry-level judicial-service designation rather than one uniform all-India cadre title. Rajasthan officially lists “Civil Judge and Judicial Magistrate” among its district courts, while Uttar Pradesh rules treat a Civil Judge (Junior Division) as including a Judicial Magistrate First Class and related postings.

quick_facts.label: Core legal basis for the combined role
quick_facts.value: Under section 9 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023, the High Court may confer the powers of a Judicial Magistrate of the first or second class on a member of a State Judicial Service who is functioning as a judge in a civil court.

quick_facts.label: Typical entry mechanism
quick_facts.value: Entry is ordinarily through the relevant state judicial-services competitive recruitment process, but the authority, eligibility conditions, examination pattern, vacancies, and dates are state- and notification-specific. For example, Uttar Pradesh’s rules provide for direct recruitment to Civil Judge (Junior Division) through a competitive examination conducted by the state commission, subject to vacancies.

quick_facts.label: Education and language example
quick_facts.value: Candidates must follow the governing state notification. As an illustration only, the Uttar Pradesh rules require an LL.B. from a recognised university or specified advocate/barrister status, plus thorough knowledge of Hindi in Devanagari; these conditions should not be assumed to apply unchanged in another state.

quick_facts.label: Institutional setting
quick_facts.value: Judicial Magistrates’ courts are established district-wise by the State Government after consultation with the High Court, and their presiding officers are appointed by the High Court. Judicial Magistrates other than the Chief Judicial Magistrate are, subject to the Sessions Judge’s general control, subordinate to the Chief Judicial Magistrate.

sections.heading: Responsibilities: civil-court judging combined with criminal-magisterial powers
paragraphs: The combined title reflects a state judicial officer who may function as a judge in a civil court and also be invested with Judicial Magistrate powers. Section 9 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (BNSS) expressly permits the High Court to confer first- or second-class Judicial Magistrate powers on a State Judicial Service member functioning as a civil-court judge. The exact allocation of civil and criminal work depends on the state’s cadre rules, posting order, local jurisdiction, and the business distribution made by the competent judicial authority.

In the criminal-magisterial capacity, a first-class Magistrate has the sentencing authority specified by BNSS section 23: imprisonment up to three years, a fine up to ₹50,000, both, or community service, subject to the statute and the facts of the case. The officer must therefore conduct proceedings, assess material placed before the court, issue reasoned judicial orders, and work within the jurisdiction and powers assigned by law.
The role is judicial, not executive-magisterial. BNSS separately identifies Judicial Magistrates and Executive Magistrates as distinct classes of criminal courts/officers.
bullets: Apply procedural and substantive law to matters assigned to the court.
Conduct hearings and make judicial orders in the civil and criminal jurisdiction lawfully conferred.
Maintain a clear record of proceedings and reasons, particularly where evidence or an accused’s examination must be recorded under the BNSS.
Operate within local jurisdiction and the administrative distribution of work under the district judiciary.

sections.heading: Entry route: state judicial-services recruitment
paragraphs: The practical route is the state-wise Judicial Services Examination (often called PCS-J or Civil Judge recruitment), followed by appointment under the applicable state judicial-service rules. Recruitment is not nationally standardised: candidates should use the relevant High Court, state public service commission, or recruiting authority’s current advertisement as the controlling document for vacancies, age, reservation, language requirements, examination scheme, fees, and deadlines.

Uttar Pradesh provides a useful official illustration of the route: direct recruitment to Civil Judge (Junior Division) is through a competitive examination conducted by the Commission; its rules describe a written examination in legal and allied subjects including procedure, language testing, and an interview. The same rules require appointed officers to undergo training during probation. These details demonstrate the structure of one state’s process, not a universal eligibility checklist.

For an applicant considering a state that uses the literal combined designation, Rajasthan’s High Court has used “Civil Judge and Judicial Magistrate” as the post label in its recruitment materials. Applicants should still rely on the latest recruitment notice rather than historical instructions or another state’s rules.
bullets: Complete a recognised legal qualification required by the recruiting state.
Track the current state judicial-services notification issued by the competent authority.
Prepare for state-prescribed written papers, language requirements where applicable, and interview/personality assessment where prescribed.
After selection, complete the probationary and judicial-training requirements specified by the state or High Court.

sections.heading: Skills signalled by the recruitment and judicial work
paragraphs: The role requires strong legal analysis and procedural command. Uttar Pradesh’s official rules, for example, test legal and allied subjects including procedure, and use an interview to assess merit, ability, character, personality, physique, and general suitability. Because a combined post may involve both civil-court work and magisterial functions, candidates need to read statutes carefully, distinguish jurisdictional limits, analyse facts and evidence, and write legally sustainable reasons.

Language and communication capability are also material but state-specific. Uttar Pradesh’s rules require thorough knowledge of Hindi in Devanagari and test Hindi, English, and Urdu in its competitive examination. Other states may prescribe different official-language requirements, so the current notification governs.

Professional integrity, independence, impartiality, careful case management, and the ability to communicate respectfully in court are essential practical capabilities for a judicial office, consistent with the statutory responsibility to ensure expeditious and proper disposal of cases in courts subordinate to the High Court.
bullets: Legal research, statutory interpretation, and command of civil and criminal procedure.
Structured judicial writing: concise orders, clear reasons, and accurate recording of proceedings.
Evidence and fact analysis, including identifying the limits of the court’s jurisdiction and powers.
Courtroom communication, listening, fairness, composure, and disciplined case management.
State-prescribed language proficiency.

sections.heading: Workplace and reporting context
paragraphs: The usual workplace is the subordinate judiciary/district-court system in the recruiting state, rather than a ministry or a police organisation. BNSS provides that the State Government establishes courts of Judicial Magistrates in districts after consultation with the High Court, while the High Court appoints their presiding officers. A posting may therefore be tied to a district, sub-division, or other local jurisdiction fixed under law.

The judicial hierarchy matters to daily work. BNSS places the Chief Judicial Magistrate under the Sessions Judge, and other Judicial Magistrates under the Chief Judicial Magistrate, subject to the Sessions Judge’s general control. The Chief Judicial Magistrate may also make rules or special orders for business distribution among subordinate magistrates. This means an officer’s criminal docket and territorial scope are determined by lawful assignment, not solely by the title of the post.

In a state using a combined civil-judge/judicial-magistrate structure, the officer may be assigned civil-court and criminal-magisterial functions at different times or in different postings. Uttar Pradesh’s recent High Court notifications illustrate that officers can be posted between Civil Judge (Junior Division), Additional Civil Judge (Junior Division), and Judicial Magistrate First Class assignments.
bullets: District and subordinate courts within the state judicial system.
Courtroom hearings, chambers-based judgment/order writing, case-record review, and administrative judicial work.
Posting, local jurisdiction, and case allocation determined by the High Court and district judicial hierarchy.
Potential movement among equivalent or related junior-division civil and magisterial assignments, according to state rules and posting orders.

sections.heading: Progression: promotion within the state judicial service
paragraphs: Career progression is controlled by the relevant state judicial-service rules and High Court decisions; it is not a single national ladder. In Uttar Pradesh, the rules identify Civil Judge (Junior Division) and Civil Judge (Senior Division) as service cadres and provide that promotion to Civil Judge (Senior Division) is made by the High Court from Civil Judge (Junior Division) officers, based on seniority-cum-merit after a selection process.

UP’s rules also show that training and performance remain relevant after entry: every newly appointed Civil Judge (Junior Division) undergoes training during probation, the normal probation period is two years, and confirmation takes account of satisfactory prescribed training and work and conduct. Other states can use different cadre names, promotion criteria, training institutions, or timelines.

At the magisterial side, BNSS provides for High Court appointment of a first-class Judicial Magistrate as Chief Judicial Magistrate and allows appointment as Additional Chief Judicial Magistrate. Whether and when an individual officer reaches those assignments depends on the applicable service rules, seniority, merit, vacancy position, and High Court orders.
bullets: Entry/junior cadre: Civil Judge (Junior Division) or the state’s equivalent combined designation.
State-specific postings may include Civil Judge, Additional Civil Judge, or Judicial Magistrate First Class functions.
Illustrative UP promotion: Civil Judge (Junior Division) to Civil Judge (Senior Division), through High Court selection on seniority-cum-merit.
Higher magisterial leadership assignments can include Chief Judicial Magistrate or Additional Chief Judicial Magistrate where appointed by the High Court.
